results.description: This variant has strong previous evidence of pathogenicity in unrelated individuals. ClinGen Lysosomal Storage Disorder VCEP classified as Pathogenic (3-star ClinVar, approved 2021-08-17) with criteria PM3_Very Strong, PM5, PS3_Moderate, PP4_Moderate, and PP3. Documented in over 50 unrelated individuals with Pompe disease (GSD type II) including more than 30 homozygotes and multiple compound heterozygous cases across China, Taiwan, Thailand, and Malaysia. Most common GAA mutation in East Asian infantile-onset Pompe disease patients (25–80% of alleles); associated with founder effect in Southern Han Chinese populations. Classic IOPD onset 1–6 months with severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (EF 9–33%), profound hypotonia, respiratory failure, and high mortality without early ERT; late-onset Pompe disease also reported in compound heterozygotes (muscle weakness onset 4 years to adolescence, no cardiac involvement). Functional studies demonstrate 0.08–0.82% GAA activity in homozygous patient fibroblasts; CRISPR knock-in mouse model confirms near-abolished enzymatic activity (~1% WT) and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. Heterozygous carriers have approximately 50% GAA activity and are unaffected.
results.notes:
**Summary:** Pathogenic. ClinVar 3-star ClinGen LSD VCEP expert panel classification (P, approved 2021-08-17), criteria applied: PM3_Very Strong (>30 homozygotes and ≥5 compound het cases with confirmed pathogenic allele in trans), PM5 (p.Asp645Asn at same codon classified P), PS3_Moderate (<10% GAA activity in transfected COS cells; <1% in homozygous patient fibroblasts), PP4_Moderate (most common variant in IOPD patients from China/Taiwan/Thailand), PP3 (REVEL 0.8). Population frequency 0.17% East Asian (gnomAD) exceeds the ClinGen LSD PM2 threshold of 0.001 — PM2 not applied — but this elevated frequency is explained by a founder allele in the Southern Han Chinese diaspora. Over 50 unrelated affected individuals documented across registry and cohort studies. No refuting evidence: heterozygous carriers without a second pathogenic allele in trans have ~50% GAA activity and are clinically unaffected.

**Note on haplotype context:** In Asian populations, c.1935C>A frequently occurs in cis with the benign pseudodeficiency variant c.1726G>A (p.Gly576Ser). Functional studies confirm D645E alone drives severe GAA enzyme deficiency; c.1935C>A is the disease-causing allele regardless of c.1726G>A co-occurrence.

**Note on 'potentially less severe' designation:** The Erasmus Pompe variant database designates c.1935C>A as 'potentially less severe,' reflecting ability to cause both IOPD and LOPD — not a challenge to ACMG pathogenic classification.

**Supporting evidence:**

- **ClinVar**: ClinGen LSD VCEP classified P (2021, 3-star), applying PM3_VS + PM5 + PS3_Moderate + PP4_Moderate + PP3. At least 50 affected individuals with reduced GAA activity documented; >30 homozygotes; ≥5 compound het cases with confirmed pathogenic allele in trans; functional studies (PMID: 8094613, 19862843) show <10% normal GAA activity and abnormal protein processing; another variant at same codon (p.Asp645Asn, ClinVar VCV000188728) classified P. 16 additional laboratory submissions all classify as Pathogenic.

- **Kan2022**: [Most frequent pathogenic variant in IOPD in Southern Chinese, Taiwanese, and Southeast Asian populations of Han ancestry](#cite:Kan2022 "the GAA c.1935C>A transversion in exon 14, which results in the p.Asp645Glu (p.D645E) missense mutation, is the most frequent pathogenic variant associated with IOPD in the Southern Chinese, Taiwanese, and Southeast Asian populations of Han ancestry, but is not frequently reported in any other region"), [representing 36–80% of mutations in Taiwanese IOPD with proposed founder effect](#cite:Kan2022 "In Taiwanese populations, this c.1935C>A variant represents 36–80% of mutations11·12, and occur in context of a specific haplotype with conserved polymorphic markers linked to Taiwanese Pompe IOPD patients comparing to normal individuals. This may suggest the existence of a founder effect stemming from a diaspora of Southern Han Chinese to Taiwan and other locations"). [Homozygous patients present neonatally with severe HCM, hypotonia, respiratory failure, and infantile mortality](#cite:Kan2022 "causes infantile-onset Pompe disease (IOPD), presenting neonatally with severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, profound muscle hypotonia, respiratory failure, and infantile mortality"). [Patient fibroblast GAA activity 0.08–0.82% of normal](#cite:Kan2022 "This aligns with observed levels of low GAA enzyme activity (0.08–0.82% of normal range for control) previously measured in homozygous GAA c.1935C>A patient fibroblasts25"). CRISPR KI mouse: [~1% WT GAA activity in heart, skeletal muscle, and brain with normal mRNA levels (no NMD)](#cite:Kan2022 "KI mice had normal Gaa mRNA levels with significantly reduced level of GAA hydrolysis activity (about 1% of WT) in heart and skeletal muscle, as well as brain tissue"); KI mice develop [HCM at ~2 months and skeletal muscle weakness](#cite:Kan2022 "Gaaem1935C>A mice developed hypertrophic cardiomyopathy at approximately two months of age, which becomes quite marked at three months of age. This muscle weakness phenotype may be due to a combination of sequelae from cardiomyopathy, impairment of lysosomal-autophagosomal fusion into autolysosomes, and catabolism of myofibril contractile proteins"). [Het mice ~50% WT GAA activity, no phenotypic abnormality](#cite:Kan2022 "the HET group had close to 50% of the level of enzymatic activity observed in the WT group in each muscle tissue and brain tissue sample tested, indicating that the one WT allele produced functional enzyme, but not the c.1935C>A allele").

- **Reuser2019**: Pompe Registry (1,079 patients): [c.1935C>A reported 42 times across phenotypic subgroups (Groups A, B, C)](#cite:Reuser2019 "exon14 | c.1935C>A | p.(Asp645Glu) | 42 | A,B,C"); [second most common globally and most common in Asia-Pacific in Group A (classic IOPD)](#cite:Reuser2019 "c.1935C>A was the second most common globally and the most common in Asia‐Pacific but was not frequently reported in any other region."); [7 homozygous CRIM-positive Group A patients](#cite:Reuser2019 "c.1935C>A (*n*=7) | | | c.1935C>A |"); [linked in cis with pseudodeficiency variant c.1726G>A in Asia-Pacific, accounting for nearly half of Group A alleles](#cite:Reuser2019 "In Asia‐Pacific, c.1935C>A was reported most often, but was not among the top five reported in other regions. Nevertheless, this variant was the fourth most common globally due to the input of patients from Asia‐Pacific where it accounted for nearly half of reported variants in Group A. Not unexpectedly, c1726G>A also was common in Group A in this region since c.1935C>A and c.1726G>A are linked in cis in patients in the Asia‐Pacific."); homozygosity for any Group A variant (including c.1935C>A) is classified as 'very severe' and expected to cause classic IOPD, confirmed by Registry homozygous genotype data.

- **Chan2023**: Malaysian IOPD cohort (n=17): [most common mutation, 8 patients (10 alleles, 33% allele frequency)](#cite:Chan2023 "The most common mutation identified was c.1935C>A p.(D645E), occurring in eight patients (10 alleles) with allele frequency of (33%)."); [2 unrelated homozygous patients](#cite:Chan2023 "Two unrelated patients were homozygous for the c.1935C>A p.(D645E) mutation. The other 13 were compound heterozygotes.") (Pt13: Chinese, GAA 2.0%, onset 4 months, HCM EF 28%, LVMI 483 g/m², CK 1100, died age 2 years; Pt17: Malay, GAA 1.7%, onset 2 months, HCM EF 29%, CK 930, died 6 months); [cohort features: HCM 100%, respiratory insufficiency 94%, hypotonia 88%, failure to thrive 82%; median presentation 3 months, median diagnosis 6 months](#cite:Chan2023 "Clinical features documented at presentation were hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (100%), respiratory weakness (94%), hypotonia (88%), failure to thrive (82%), feeding difficulties (76%) and hepatomegaly (76%). Our cohort had a median age at presentation of 3 months. The median age of diagnosis was 6 months"); [most common mutation in Southern China (25%), Taiwan (36%), Thailand (32.4%)](#cite:Chan2023 "The most frequently identified mutation in our cohort is c.1935C>A p.(D645E), with an allele frequency of 33%. This has also been reported to be the most common mutation in Southern China (25%), Taiwan (36%) and Thailand (32.4%)").

- **Hsueh2021**: 19 NBS-confirmed IOPD Taiwanese patients; at least 6 homozygous for c.1935C>A + c.1726G>A haplotype and 7+ compound het patients with c.1935C>A. [ERT initiated at mean 11.05±4.31 days (range 6–23 days); hearing impairment in 31.6% at follow-up](#cite:Hsueh2021 "The mean age of the initial time to ERT was 11.05±4.31 days, and the HI rate was 31.6% (6/19).").

- **Chen2024**: 7 of 8 Pompe disease patients carry c.1935C>A in cis with c.1726G>A as haplotype c.[1935C>A;1726G>A]. [5 IOPD patients (B1–B5) diagnosed 8 days–3 months of age with significant cardiac complications](#cite:Chen2024 "The remaining five were diagnosed with classic IOPD between 8 days and three months of age, primarily presenting with significant cardiac complications."); [LOPD patients (A2, A3) had muscle weakness onset early adolescence and age 4 years, no cardiac involvement](#cite:Chen2024 "Three patients were diagnosed with LOPD (Subject A1-A3) with muscle weakness noted during early adolescence, early infancy, and four years of age, respectively. None exhibited any cardiac manifestations.").

- **Zhu2022**: [4 of 10 Chinese IOPD patients carry c.1935C>A, all compound het, all CRIM-positive](#cite:Zhu2022 "The most frequent mutation observed in our study was c.1935C > A, with four out of ten patients having this mutation."); onset 4–6 months; severe cardiomyopathy (LVMI 231.8–745.5 g/m²); all GAA activity below normal range.

- **Ngiwsara2019**: Thai IOPD patient (Pt2): [cHet c.1935C>A/c.1933G>C (p.Asp645His), undetectable GAA activity (0%), onset 1–2 months with tachypnea, BVH, EF 30%; favorable outcome at 4 years on ERT started age 4 weeks](#cite:Ngiwsara2019 "2 | 1-2 m | 1-2 m | tachypnea, mild hepatomegaly | short PR interval, BVH, EF 30% | yes, since 4 wk./ favorable outcome at 4 y | no, but two previous deceased children with cardiomyopathy"). [In vitro studies confirmed significant GAA activity loss; p.Asp645Glu causes delayed intracellular transport, deficient phosphorylation, and proteolytic processing](#cite:Ngiwsara2019 "Previous in vitro expression studies of the mutations p.Gly293Arg, p.Trp367Arg, p.Asp645Glu, and p.Cys647Trp have confirmed significant loss of GAA activity and these variants were found in IOPD patients [23, 31–33]. The p.Asp645Glu variant has been shown to cause delayed intracellular transport, deficient phosphorylation and proteolytic processing [25]."); [prevalence of p.Asp645Glu/His 32.4% (11/34 alleles) in Thai IOPD](#cite:Ngiwsara2019 "Including the data from the present study, the prevalence of p.Asp645Glu/His is refined to 32.4% (11/34) and exon 14 was the hot spot for GAA mutation at 50% (17/34)").

- **Li2024**: Pt1 (female, cHet c.1935C>A/c.1669A>T): [onset 4 months, died 11 months with muscle weakness and cardiomegaly](#cite:Li2024 "1 | F | 4 | 10 | 11 | N | Muscle weakness, cardiomegaly"); Pt8 (male, hom c.1935C>A): [onset 1 month, died 3 months with recurrent airway infections, cardiomegaly, heart failure](#cite:Li2024 "8 | M | 1 | 3 | 3 | N | Recurrent airway infections, cardiomegaly, heart failure"). [c.1935C>A accounts for 20–25% of mutant alleles in patients from southern China](#cite:Li2024 "The most common mutation in patients from southern China was c.1C > A (p.Asp 645Glu), accounting for 20%–25% of the total mutant alleles (20).").

- **Xu2019**: [Homozygous c.1935C>A IOPD patient with onset 1 month and zero GAA activity](#cite:Xu2019 "homozygotic mutation of c.1935C>A (potentially less severe)"); compound het patients with juvenile-onset (15–47 months). [c.1935C>A reported as the most common mutation in CRIM-positive IOPD: 12/14 patients in one series (Yang et al.)](#cite:Xu2019 "Yang et al. [31] who reported on 14 infantile-onset patients who were CRIM-positive showed that 12/14 patients had the most commonly found mutation--c.1935 C > A").

- **Sawada2021**: c.1935C>A listed as ['Pathogenic' in ClinVar Miner, 'Probably damaging (1.000)' by PolyPhen-2, 'Potentially less severe' in the Pompe variant database](#cite:Sawada2021 "55 | rs28940868 | c.1935C>A | p.D645E | Exon 14 | Potentially less severe | Pathogenic | Probably damaging (1.000)"); [5 of 6 IOPD patients in Taiwan were homozygous or heterozygous for c.1935C>A](#cite:Sawada2021 "In Taiwan, five of six IOPD patients are homozygous or heterozygous for the same variant c.1935C>A.").

- **Ron2025**: Two PA NBS newborns with c.1935C>A in trans with pseudodeficiency alleles classified as carriers; [carriers have ~50% normal GAA activity and no affected carriers have been reported](#cite:Ron2025 "Carriers have about 50% of the average normal enzyme activity [6]; to our knowledge, there are no reports of affected carriers.") — confirms monoallelic c.1935C>A does not cause disease.

- **Sawada2020**: [c.1935C>A detected in all 5 confirmed IOPD cases in a Taiwan NBS program of 191,786 newborns](#cite:Sawada2020 "GAA gene analysis demonstrated that c.1935C>A (p.D645E) was detected in all cases of IOPD, c.[752C>T; 761C>T] (p.[S251L; S254L]) was detected in 8 cases of LOPD, and the c.1726G>A (p.G576S) pseudodeficiency variant was detected in 2 cases of IOPD and 5 cases of LOPD."); [classified as a common IOPD-associated variant in two large Taiwan NBS programs](#cite:Sawada2020 "c.1935C>A (p.D645E), a common missense variant in Taiwan [6], and c.2560C>T (p.R854*), a common nonsense variant in Africa [57], are considered variants for IOPD.").

- **Moriyoshimura2025**: c.1935C>A (in haplotype c.[1935C>A;1726G>A]) identified in 4 IOPD patients in the Mini-COMET trial ([2 in Cohort 1](#cite:Moriyoshimura2025 "c.[784G > A];[1935C > A;1726G > A], c.[1062C > G;1286 A > G];[1935C > A;1726G > A], c.[1222 A > G];[1927G > A], c.[236_246del];[655G > A], c.[1798C > T];[1798C > T], c.[1000G > T];[271G > A;307 T > G]"), [2 in Cohort 2 including 1 homozygous for the haplotype](#cite:Moriyoshimura2025 "c.[1935C > A;1726G > A];[2842INST], c.[1935C > A;1726G > A];[1935C > A;1726G > A], c.[1193del];[1927G > A], c.[2015G > A];[2015G > A], c.[2646 + 2 T > A]")); all ERT-experienced IOPD patients aged 1–12 years with prior clinical decline.

- **Liu2021**: Taiwanese female infant (4 months), cHet c.1935C>A/c.424_440del17 (p.Ser142Leufs*29): [GAA activity 0.04 μM/h (normal >0.80), CK 872, AST 125 U/L, cardiomegaly; ERT started day 15, LVMI normalized from 105.49 to 65.5 g/m² by 4 months](#cite:Liu2021 "After birth, she presented symptoms of mild hypotonia without dysphagia or dysarthria. Occasional shortness of breath was noticed. Laboratory data revealed abnormally high aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), and creatinine kinase (CK), which were 125 U/L (normal range: <32 U/L), 48 U/L (normal range: <33 U/L), and 872 U/L (normal range: 26–192 U/L), respectively. The enzyme activity test of GAA was 0.04 μM/h (normal range: >0.80 μM/h).").

- **Huang2023**: iPSC models from IOPD patients carrying c.1935C>A (homozygous or cHet with c.2040+1G>T) demonstrated [glycogen accumulation, GAA enzyme deficiency, and ultrastructural aberrances in derived cardiomyocytes, alleviated by rhGAA treatment](#cite:Huang2023 "Huang et al. (25) established iPSCs from two patients with late-onset Pompe disease (LOPD-iPSCs) carrying GAA mutations and derived cardiomyocytes (PD-iCM) from PD-iPSC. It was shown that a mass of glycogen accumulated in PD-iCM, underlying the ultrastructural aberrances including swollen mitochondria, the formation of vacuoles containing glycogen particles, and the formation of autophagosome-like structures. The above major pathologic phenotypes of PD-iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes were alleviated by rhGAA (25).").

papers.rank_rationale: Provides the most comprehensive functional evidence including patient fibroblast data (0.08–0.82% GAA activity), CRISPR mouse model with ~1% WT activity and HCM phenotype, and population context as the most frequent IOPD variant in East Asian populations.
Largest Pompe disease dataset (1,079 patients from the Pompe Registry), documenting 42 occurrences of c.1935C>A including 7 homozygous IOPD cases across phenotypic subgroups.
Largest single cohort study specifically characterizing c.1935C>A in 8 Malaysian IOPD patients with detailed per-patient clinical, enzymatic, and outcome data including 2 unrelated homozygotes.
Documents 19 NBS-confirmed Taiwanese IOPD patients carrying c.1935C>A with very early ERT initiation at mean 11 days and longitudinal hearing outcome data.
Documents 7 of 8 Pompe disease patients carrying c.1935C>A and directly contrasts IOPD and LOPD phenotypic spectrum in haplotype carriers.
Documents 4 Chinese IOPD patients with c.1935C>A and severe cardiomyopathy, all compound het and CRIM-positive, with detailed biochemical and echocardiographic data.
Documents a Thai IOPD patient compound het for c.1935C>A at codon 645 and summarizes in vitro functional evidence for delayed transport and deficient proteolytic processing.
Documents 2 Chinese Pompe disease patients (1 compound het, 1 homozygous) with c.1935C>A and fatal infantile-onset presentations, confirming high allele frequency in southern China.
Documents homozygous and compound heterozygous c.1935C>A patients across IOPD and LOPD spectrum including one homozygous case with zero GAA activity.
Provides variant classification context in Japanese NBS program and Taiwan cohort data, clarifying the 'potentially less severe' vs 'Pathogenic' classification distinction.
Provides carrier data confirming that monoallelic c.1935C>A results in ~50% GAA activity and no clinical disease, supporting autosomal recessive mode.
Documents c.1935C>A detection in all confirmed IOPD cases across large Taiwan NBS programs, establishing its role as a defining IOPD allele.
Documents c.1935C>A in IOPD patients enrolled in a Japanese clinical trial, providing independent corroboration in an interventional study setting.
Provides detailed single case report of a Taiwanese IOPD infant with compound heterozygous c.1935C>A, markedly reduced GAA activity, and response to early ERT.
iPSC model review providing functional corroboration of c.1935C>A pathogenicity in derived cardiomyocytes and neural cells from affected patients.

claims.text: c.1935C>A (p.D645E) is the most frequent pathogenic variant associated with IOPD in Southern Chinese, Taiwanese, and Southeast Asian populations of Han ancestry, but is not frequently reported in any other region.

citations.quote: the GAA c.1935C>A transversion in exon 14, which results in the p.Asp645Glu (p.D645E) missense mutation, is the most frequent pathogenic variant associated with IOPD in the Southern Chinese, Taiwanese, and Southeast Asian populations of Han ancestry, but is not frequently reported in any other region

claims.text: In Taiwanese IOPD patients, c.1935C>A represents 36–80% of mutations and occurs on a specific haplotype, suggesting a founder effect from a diaspora of Southern Han Chinese to Taiwan and other locations.

citations.quote: In Taiwanese populations, this c.1935C>A variant represents 36–80% of mutations11·12, and occur in context of a specific haplotype with conserved polymorphic markers linked to Taiwanese Pompe IOPD patients comparing to normal individuals. This may suggest the existence of a founder effect stemming from a diaspora of Southern Han Chinese to Taiwan and other locations

claims.text: Human IOPD caused by homozygous c.1935C>A presents neonatally with severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, profound muscle hypotonia, respiratory failure, and infantile mortality.

citations.quote: causes infantile-onset Pompe disease (IOPD), presenting neonatally with severe hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, profound muscle hypotonia, respiratory failure, and infantile mortality

claims.text: GAA enzymatic activity in homozygous GAA c.1935C>A patient fibroblasts was previously measured at 0.08–0.82% of the normal range for controls.

citations.quote: This aligns with observed levels of low GAA enzyme activity (0.08–0.82% of normal range for control) previously measured in homozygous GAA c.1935C>A patient fibroblasts25

claims.text: CRISPR knock-in mouse model homozygous for c.1935C>A showed ~1% WT GAA activity in heart, skeletal muscle, and brain, with normal Gaa mRNA levels (no NMD), developed HCM at ~2 months of age, and showed skeletal muscle weakness.

citations.quote: KI mice had normal Gaa mRNA levels with significantly reduced level of GAA hydrolysis activity (about 1% of WT) in heart and skeletal muscle, as well as brain tissue

The comparative ΔCt between mouse Gaa and housekeeping gene Gapdh acquired by RT-PCR among WT, HET, and KI groups are almost identical, indicating the Gaa c.1935C>A mutation does not affect Gaa mRNA levels

Gaaem1935C>A mice developed hypertrophic cardiomyopathy at approximately two months of age, which becomes quite marked at three months of age. This muscle weakness phenotype may be due to a combination of sequelae from cardiomyopathy, impairment of lysosomal-autophagosomal fusion into autolysosomes, and catabolism of myofibril contractile proteins
the male Gaaem1935C>A KI mouse showed a significant reduction (~19%) in normalized peak tension force compared to WT mice, indicating decreased forelimb muscle strength in KI mice

claims.text: Heterozygous Gaa c.1935C>A knock-in mice had ~50% WT GAA activity with no phenotypic abnormality, consistent with single-allele carrier status.

citations.quote: the HET group had close to 50% of the level of enzymatic activity observed in the WT group in each muscle tissue and brain tissue sample tested, indicating that the one WT allele produced functional enzyme, but not the c.1935C>A allele

claims.text: c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) was reported 42 times across phenotypic subgroups (Groups A, B, and C) in the Pompe Registry dataset of 1,079 patients.

citations.quote: exon14 | c.1935C>A | p.(Asp645Glu) | 42 | A,B,C

claims.text: c.1935C>A was the second most common variant globally and the most common in Asia-Pacific in Group A (classic IOPD, onset ≤12 months with cardiomyopathy), but was not frequently reported in other regions.

citations.quote: c.1935C>A was the second most common globally and the most common in Asia‐Pacific but was not frequently reported in any other region.

claims.text: c.1935C>A was found in homozygosity in 7 CRIM-positive Group A (classic IOPD) patients in the Pompe Registry.
In Asia-Pacific, c.1935C>A is linked in cis with pseudodeficiency variant c.1726G>A and accounts for nearly half of reported Group A variants; c.1935C>A is the fourth most common variant globally.

citations.quote: In Asia‐Pacific, c.1935C>A was reported most often, but was not among the top five reported in other regions. Nevertheless, this variant was the fourth most common globally due to the input of patients from Asia‐Pacific where it accounted for nearly half of reported variants in Group A. Not unexpectedly, c1726G>A also was common in Group A in this region since c.1935C>A and c.1726G>A are linked in cis in patients in the Asia‐Pacific.

claims.text: Group A variants (including c.1935C>A) are classified as 'very severe' and homozygosity for any of them is expected to cause classic IOPD, confirmed by homozygous genotype data in the Registry.

citations.quote: With regard to the severity of *GAA* variants, those identified in Group A (Table 5) are "very severe" as they were found in patients with very early onset of first symptoms (The Human Phenotype Ontology (HPO), 2019) and cardiomyopathy (HP:0001638), that is, patients with classic infantile Pompe disease. Homozygosity for any of these variants is expected to lead to this phenotype, and this expectation is largely confirmed by the data on homozygous genotypes presented in Table 6.

claims.text: c.1935C>A p.(D645E) is the most common mutation in the Malaysian IOPD cohort, found in 8 patients (10 alleles) with an allele frequency of 33%.

citations.quote: The most common mutation identified was c.1935C>A p.(D645E), occurring in eight patients (10 alleles) with allele frequency of (33%).

claims.text: Two unrelated patients in the Malaysian cohort were homozygous for c.1935C>A p.(D645E); the remaining patients carrying this variant were compound heterozygotes.

citations.quote: Two unrelated patients were homozygous for the c.1935C>A p.(D645E) mutation. The other 13 were compound heterozygotes.

claims.text: Patient 13 (Chinese, homozygous c.1935C>A) had GAA activity 2.0%, onset at 4 months, HCM (EF 28%, LVMI 483 g/m²), CK 1100 U/L, heart failure, and died at age 2 years.
Patient 17 (Malay, homozygous c.1935C>A) had GAA activity 1.7%, onset at 2 months, HCM (EF 29%), CK 930 U/L, and died at 6 months.

The Malaysian IOPD cohort presented with HCM (100%), respiratory insufficiency (94%), hypotonia (88%), failure to thrive (82%), feeding difficulties (76%), and hepatomegaly (76%); median age at presentation 3 months, median diagnosis 6 months.

citations.quote: Clinical features documented at presentation were hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (100%), respiratory weakness (94%), hypotonia (88%), failure to thrive (82%), feeding difficulties (76%) and hepatomegaly (76%). Our cohort had a median age at presentation of 3 months. The median age of diagnosis was 6 months

claims.text: c.1935C>A p.(D645E) is the most common mutation in Southern China (25%), Taiwan (36%), and Thailand (32.4%).

citations.quote: The most frequently identified mutation in our cohort is c.1935C>A p.(D645E), with an allele frequency of 33%. This has also been reported to be the most common mutation in Southern China (25%), Taiwan (36%) and Thailand (32.4%)

claims.text: All 19 NBS-confirmed IOPD patients received ERT at mean 11.05±4.31 days (range 6–23 days); hearing impairment rate was 31.6% at last follow-up.

citations.quote: The mean age of the initial time to ERT was 11.05±4.31 days, and the HI rate was 31.6% (6/19).
confirmatory diagnoses using GAA gene sequencing was usually performed in 5 days passing their newborn hearing screenings

claims.text: Patient 2 (male, GA 38w, ERT started day 15) carries c.1935C>A (p.D645E) homozygously with c.1726G>A (p.G576S) homozygously (IOPD by NBS), and developed mild single-ear SNHL at age 6.7 years.

claims.text: Seven of eight Pompe disease patients carry c.1935C>A in cis with c.1726G>A as haplotype c.[1935C>A;1726G>A].

citations.quote: Apart from Subject A1, all carried at least one allele with the c.1935C>A (p.D645E) variant in the GAA gene.

claims.text: Five IOPD patients (B1–B5) carrying c.[1935C>A;1726G>A] were diagnosed with classic IOPD between 8 days and 3 months of age, primarily presenting with significant cardiac complications.

citations.quote: The remaining five were diagnosed with classic IOPD between 8 days and three months of age, primarily presenting with significant cardiac complications.

claims.text: LOPD patients (A2, A3) carrying c.[1935C>A;1726G>A] had muscle weakness onset during early adolescence and at age 4 years, with no cardiac manifestations.

citations.quote: Three patients were diagnosed with LOPD (Subject A1-A3) with muscle weakness noted during early adolescence, early infancy, and four years of age, respectively. None exhibited any cardiac manifestations.

claims.text: Patient B4 (IOPD, NBS-diagnosed) is homozygous for the c.[1935C>A;1726G>A] haplotype, diagnosed at ~1 month of age.

c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu) was the most frequent GAA mutation in this Chinese IOPD cohort, present in 4 of 10 patients, all compound het and CRIM-positive; all patients had GAA activity below the lower limit of normal.

citations.quote: The most frequent mutation observed in our study was c.1935C > A, with four out of ten patients having this mutation.
All patients had GAA enzyme test results below the lower limit of the normal reference range.

claims.text: Patient 0104 (female, cHet c.1935C>A/c.1432G>A, onset 6 months) had GAA activity 0.37 μmol/L/h (normal 2.88–98.02), LVMI 745.5 g/m², LVEF 49.2%, severe IOPD phenotype.

Thai IOPD patient 2 carries c.1935C>A compound heterozygous with c.1933G>C (p.Asp645His); undetectable GAA activity (0%); onset 1–2 months with tachypnea, BVH, EF 30%; favorable outcome at 4 years on ERT started age 4 weeks.

claims.text: Previous in vitro expression studies confirmed significant loss of GAA activity for p.Asp645Glu; the variant causes delayed intracellular transport, deficient phosphorylation, and proteolytic processing.

citations.quote: Previous in vitro expression studies of the mutations p.Gly293Arg, p.Trp367Arg, p.Asp645Glu, and p.Cys647Trp have confirmed significant loss of GAA activity and these variants were found in IOPD patients [23, 31–33]. The p.Asp645Glu variant has been shown to cause delayed intracellular transport, deficient phosphorylation and proteolytic processing [25].

claims.text: Prevalence of p.Asp645Glu/His in Thai IOPD patients refined to 32.4% (11/34 alleles); exon 14 is a mutation hotspot at 50% (17/34 alleles).

citations.quote: Including the data from the present study, the prevalence of p.Asp645Glu/His is refined to 32.4% (11/34) and exon 14 was the hot spot for GAA mutation at 50% (17/34)

claims.text: c.1935C>A accounts for 20–25% of mutant alleles in patients from southern China, making it the most common mutation in that region.

citations.quote: The most common mutation in patients from southern China was c.1C > A (p.Asp 645Glu), accounting for 20%–25% of the total mutant alleles (20).

claims.text: c.1935C>A is reported as the most commonly found mutation in CRIM-positive infantile-onset Pompe disease patients: 12 of 14 such patients in one series (Yang et al.).

citations.quote: Yang et al. [31] who reported on 14 infantile-onset patients who were CRIM-positive showed that 12/14 patients had the most commonly found mutation--c.1935 C > A

claims.text: c.1935C>A is classified as 'Pathogenic' in ClinVar Miner and 'Probably damaging (1.000)' by PolyPhen-2; designated 'Potentially less severe' in the Pompe disease GAA variant database (reflecting ability to cause both IOPD and LOPD).

claims.text: In Taiwan, 5 of 6 IOPD patients were homozygous or heterozygous for c.1935C>A; the high IOPD frequency in Taiwan is attributed to this allele.

citations.quote: In Taiwan, five of six IOPD patients are homozygous or heterozygous for the same variant c.1935C>A.
The high frequency of IOPD patients in Taiwan is attributed to the high frequency of allele c.1935C>A, which was most commonly found in IOPD patients in Taiwan and southern China [22–24].

claims.text: Two Pennsylvania NBS newborns with c.1935C>A in trans with pseudodeficiency alleles were classified as carriers; carriers have ~50% normal enzyme activity and no affected carriers have been reported.

citations.quote: Carriers have about 50% of the average normal enzyme activity [6]; to our knowledge, there are no reports of affected carriers.

claims.text: c.1935C>A (p.D645E) was detected in all 5 confirmed IOPD cases in a Taiwan NBS program of 191,786 newborns; classified as a common IOPD-associated variant in two large Taiwan NBS programs.

citations.quote: GAA gene analysis demonstrated that c.1935C>A (p.D645E) was detected in all cases of IOPD, c.[752C>T; 761C>T] (p.[S251L; S254L]) was detected in 8 cases of LOPD, and the c.1726G>A (p.G576S) pseudodeficiency variant was detected in 2 cases of IOPD and 5 cases of LOPD.
c.1935C>A (p.D645E), a common missense variant in Taiwan [6], and c.2560C>T (p.R854*), a common nonsense variant in Africa [57], are considered variants for IOPD.

claims.text: c.1935C>A (in haplotype c.[1935C>A;1726G>A]) was identified in 4 IOPD patients in the Mini-COMET clinical trial (2 in Cohort 1, 2 in Cohort 2 including 1 homozygous for the haplotype); all ERT-experienced IOPD patients aged 1–12 years with prior clinical decline.

Taiwanese female infant (4 months), cHet c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu)/c.424_440del17, confirmed IOPD: GAA activity 0.04 μM/h (normal >0.80), CK 872, AST 125 U/L, cardiomegaly; ERT started day 15, LVMI normalized from 105.49 to 65.5 g/m² by 4 months.

citations.quote: GAA gene sequencing was performed, and the patient had two pathogenic heterozygous mutations, c.424_440del17 (p.Ser142Leufs*29) and c.1935C>A (p.Asp645Glu).

After birth, she presented symptoms of mild hypotonia without dysphagia or dysarthria. Occasional shortness of breath was noticed. Laboratory data revealed abnormally high aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), and creatinine kinase (CK), which were 125 U/L (normal range: <32 U/L), 48 U/L (normal range: <33 U/L), and 872 U/L (normal range: 26–192 U/L), respectively. The enzyme activity test of GAA was 0.04 μM/h (normal range: >0.80 μM/h).

claims.text: iPSC models from IOPD patients carrying c.1935C>A (homozygous or cHet with c.2040+1G>T) demonstrated glycogen accumulation, GAA enzyme deficiency, and ultrastructural aberrances in derived cardiomyocytes and neural cells, alleviated by rhGAA treatment.

citations.quote: IOPD | Retrovirus | Cardiomyocytes | Cellular glycogen content; Enzyme activity assay; Measurement of intact cellular respiration; Electron microscopy; Microarray analysis | Glycogen accumulation; GAA enzyme activity; Ultrastructural aberrances; Metabolomics changes | rhGAA L-carnitine | c.1935C>A c.1935C>A/c.2040+1G>T | (25)

Huang et al. (25) established iPSCs from two patients with late-onset Pompe disease (LOPD-iPSCs) carrying GAA mutations and derived cardiomyocytes (PD-iCM) from PD-iPSC. It was shown that a mass of glycogen accumulated in PD-iCM, underlying the ultrastructural aberrances including swollen mitochondria, the formation of vacuoles containing glycogen particles, and the formation of autophagosome-like structures. The above major pathologic phenotypes of PD-iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes were alleviated by rhGAA (25).

results.classification_rationale: ClinGen LSD VCEP 3-star expert panel classified as Pathogenic (2021), applying PM3_Very Strong (>30 homozygotes, ≥5 compound het cases with confirmed second pathogenic allele), PM5, PS3_Moderate (<10% GAA activity in functional studies), PP4_Moderate (most common IOPD variant in East Asian populations), and PP3 (REVEL 0.8). Over 50 unrelated affected individuals documented across multiple cohorts; consistent severe IOPD phenotype in homozygotes and compound heterozygotes; CRISPR mouse model and iPSC studies corroborate pathogenic mechanism. Heterozygous carriers are unaffected, confirming autosomal recessive inheritance.
